title: The United States Is Now a Guarantor of Last Resort
gist: Amir Handjani (Quincy Institute) argues the 2026 Iran war revealed a durable division of labor: the Gulf states, Turkey, and Pakistan now manage Iran diplomatically and militarily themselves, leaving Washington to act as guarantor of last resort rather than manager of first resort — a shift the Gulf capitals are enforcing partly by rebuffing Trump's push for Abraham Accords normalization.

themes.theme: Guarantor of last resort, not manager of first resort

insights.claim: In the 2026 Iran war, Gulf states working with Turkey and Pakistan took over the diplomatic burden of managing Iran and a share of the security burden, provided Washington remained a guarantor of last resort rather than manager of first resort.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: Marks a structural shift in US Middle East commitments — a durable regional burden-sharing arrangement that could free US bandwidth for the Indo-Pacific or Western Hemisphere, worth tracking for defense-spending and alliance-related positioning.
insights.quote: provided Washington stayed guarantor of last resort, not manager of first resort
insights.locator: The 2026 Iran war suggested a different outcome

insights.claim: The 2026 war produced a concrete division of labor: Doha, Riyadh, and Islamabad handled diplomacy with Tehran that no US administration could do credibly, while Turkey, Egypt, and Saudi Arabia floated a consortium to manage the Strait of Hormuz without US ownership.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: A Hormuz-management consortium outside direct US control would be a major shift for oil-transit security guarantees investors price into energy and shipping risk premia.
insights.quote: Turkey, Egypt, and Saudi Arabia have floated a consortium to manage Hormuz without U.S. ownership.
insights.locator: The war produced a division of labor

insights.claim: A Saudi-Pakistan defense pact signed roughly five months before the 2026 Iran war gave Pakistan a conventional military backstop that let Riyadh treat deterrence of Iran as a job it could delegate regionally rather than lean entirely on Washington for.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: A formal Saudi-Pakistan defense pact is a new regional security asset; watch for expanded Pakistani military and economic ties to Gulf capital as a knock-on trade.
insights.locator: Pakistan, bound to Saudi Arabia by a defense pact signed five months earlier

insights.claim: Even as Gulf states absorb more of the diplomatic and security burden, their militaries still depend on American parts, munitions, and training to function, which preserves US leverage even as its direct battlefield role shrinks.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: US arms and sustainment dependency is the mechanism that keeps American leverage intact despite a lower-profile posture — a bullish read-through for US defense exporters even inside a 'pullback' narrative.
insights.quote: the militaries that are taking on this burden still depend on American parts, munitions, and training to keep functioning
insights.locator: Together these interests describe an order

themes.theme: The Gulf becomes an independent player

insights.claim: In May 2015, GCC rulers snubbed Obama's Camp David summit over a JCPOA negotiated without them, but in May 2026 Trump said he held off on further strikes on Iran because Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE asked him to give negotiations more room — a reversal showing the Gulf now shapes US Iran policy rather than merely reacting to it.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: A direct before/after data point for how much bargaining power Gulf capitals now hold over US Iran policy — relevant to positioning around any future US-Iran escalation.
insights.locator: Look back to 2015 for the contrast

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia opened its own direct diplomatic channel with Iran in 2023 because the JCPOA solved only the enrichment problem Washington cared about while ignoring Iranian missiles, IRGC proxies, and a sanctions-relief windfall that flowed to Hezbollah and the Houthis.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: Explains why Gulf states no longer trust Washington-brokered nuclear deals to address their actual security concerns — a durable source of Saudi-Iran bilateral diplomacy independent of US mediation.
insights.locator: Gulf states no longer need Washington to mediate with Tehran

insights.claim: Chinese mediation did not stop Iran from striking Saudi pipelines and airbases when the war began in February 2026, but it did give Beijing a direct channel into Tehran that proved useful for de-escalation later in the conflict.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: Shows the limits of Chinese diplomatic leverage as a deterrent even as it retains value as a back-channel — tempers any 'China as new Gulf security guarantor' narrative.

insights.claim: Unconfirmed but plausible reports describe Qatar offering to curb its own gas output if Iran spared the Ras Laffan complex, and the UAE releasing billions of dollars in frozen Iranian assets in exchange for being left alone during the war; both governments denied it.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: If true, these are direct bilateral side-payments to Iran outside any US-brokered framework — worth monitoring for future Gulf-Iran deal-making that could affect gas output and sanctioned-asset flows.
insights.locator: Reports described Qatar offering to curb

themes.theme: A fractured Gulf position that still adds up to leverage

insights.claim: The Gulf Cooperation Council has never had a unified Iran policy — the UAE sought reparations from Iran and walked out of OPEC mid-crisis in a dispute with Riyadh over oil policy, Qatar and Oman argued for dialogue throughout the fighting, and Saudi Arabia expelled Iranian diplomats in March while still backing Pakistani mediation — yet together these divergent moves helped end the war.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: A fractured coalition still produced a functional outcome; the UAE's mid-crisis OPEC walkout is itself worth watching as a signal on Gulf oil-supply discipline going forward.
insights.locator: None of this adds up to a common Gulf position

insights.claim: Gulf states have unanimously rejected Trump's push to make Abraham Accords normalization the price of winding down the war: Saudi Arabia insists on real movement toward Palestinian statehood first, and Pakistan called the idea incompatible with its own principles.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: Signals Abraham Accords expansion is stalled despite US pressure — investors betting on near-term Saudi-Israel normalization should discount that timeline.
insights.quote: no normalization with Israel without real movement toward Palestinian statehood
insights.locator: The one place Gulf states have held firm

themes.theme: Second-order effects on Israel and Iran's regional ceiling

insights.claim: A Washington genuinely freed from Middle East crisis management would be less inclined to underwrite open-ended Israeli military options, which would give Israel more reason to pursue a diplomatic settlement with the Palestinians as a safer path to security than continued force.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: A second-order thesis worth watching: reduced unconditional US military backing could be a structural push factor toward Israeli-Palestinian negotiations, relevant to regional-stability-linked assets.
insights.locator: This same realignment could extend to Israel

insights.claim: The UAE absorbed more missiles and drones during the war than any other country, including Israel, and despite having two formal security partners (the US, and Israel since the Abraham Accords), neither could fully insulate it from Iran across the narrow Gulf strait — pushing Abu Dhabi toward accommodation with Tehran by the war's end.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: The UAE's outsized exposure plus Dubai's long-standing financial ties to Iran-linked business make it the Gulf state most likely to lead economic re-engagement with Tehran — a lead indicator for regional trade-normalization plays.
insights.quote: The UAE absorbed more missiles and drones during the war than any other country including Israel
insights.locator: Riyadh can live with an Iran that talks directly

insights.claim: Divergent interests among Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Turkey, and Pakistan — Turkey wants to contain rather than welcome Iranian influence in Syria and Iraq, and Pakistan's own Iran border plus its Saudi-hedging defense pact limit how far it can champion Iran — put a structural ceiling on how far Iran's reintegration into the regional order can go, even though a less-isolated Iran is what a sustainable Gulf system needs.
insights.speaker: Amir Handjani
insights.so_what: Sets realistic bounds for any Iran-reintegration trade or investment thesis — expect economic opening but continued military containment, not full normalization.
insights.locator: Together these interests describe an order
